---
title: "Chapter 1: Bear Cub (Cape Nome placer Au)"
subtitle: "OCR + 3-D modeling of 24 paper drill logs from a family heritage mining claim"
---

[← back to portfolio map](index.qmd)

## What this is

Bear Cub is a patented placer claim on the Nome paleo-beach deposit
known as the Third Beach Line, on the Seward Peninsula in
north-western Alaska. The family has held it since the early twentieth century. Its drilling history covers three operators
across four form-types between 1919 and 1955, producing twenty-four
paper drill logs that had never been transcribed. I turned those
logs into a structured geospatial dataset with quantified resource
estimates and a three-dimensional model of the bedrock surface and
pay zone.

![Bear Cub MS 1178 on satellite imagery, drill holes colored by pay-zone average grade.](data/derived/bear_cub_resource/fig_aerial_pay_zone_grade.png)

## What got built

**OCR pipeline.** I built a multi-engine pipeline with arbitration:
Tesseract for printed form headers, Hugging Face TrOCR for
handwritten depth and lithology columns, Google Cloud Vision for
the red-pencil supervisor corrections layered on top of the
original ink, and Claude Opus 4.5 with structured-output JSON as
the final arbiter. The four upstream engines each produce a
candidate per cell; Claude selects the most plausible value given
the column's expected unit, and flags cells where the engines
disagree more than the column's measurement uncertainty for manual
review.

**Schema-aware ingestion.** The four operator-specific form
conventions disagree about what the same field on the same
physical form means. Convention C (1936 Hammon field log) records
weights only at the sample level on a back page; the front page
has color counts but no weights. Without recovering and
distributing the back-page sample totals across intervals via a
color-weighted proportional rule, every Convention C hole comes
back at zero grade and the property-total resource is halved. I
implemented the recovery rule as a post-OCR step. The full
breakdown of how historical placer drill logs encode their data
lives at
[`research/bear_cub_placer_drill_log_anatomy.md`](https://johnsondevco.com/plans/ai-minerals-internal/notebooks/bear_cub/main.html#archive-and-conventions).

**Resource estimation.** I used a Voronoi polygon block model:
tessellate the property's four-corner quadrilateral into cells
around drill-hole positions, multiply cell area × pay-zone depth ×
hole grade, sum over cells. A Monte Carlo wrapper propagates three
uncertainty sources (gold fineness, OCR transcription noise,
bit-area uncertainty) over 1,000 samples to produce a 90%
uncertainty interval. The interval centers at 9,631 fine oz. An
independent recent mining-engineer analysis of the same property,
using the same polygon-block pay-zone-only methodology, lands at
10,056 oz, within about 4%.

**3-D model.** I rendered a PyVista mesh of the implied bedrock
surface and pay-zone horizon, plus a fence diagram in profile view.
The dark band immediately above the bedrock surface shows the
Third Beach Line geometry the historic operators were chasing.
Five specific holes sit on that line and account for most of the
property's gold.

![Fence diagram, west-to-east. Dark cells above the brown bedrock line are bedrock-contact pay.](data/derived/bear_cub_resource/fig_grade_fence.png)

## Where to drill next

The 24 historical Bear Cub holes are sparse: the property is about
597,000 ft², and the holes cluster along three roughly east-west
lines. Given that grid, where should hole #25 go? I treated this as
a Bayesian-experimental-design question.

The model is a Gaussian-process regressor with an anisotropic RBF
kernel on (x, y) → predicted pay-zone grade. Two acquisition
functions:

- **Maximum predictive variance** picks the location maximizing
  the GP's posterior variance. The idea is to drill where the
  model is most uncertain, because that's where a new
  measurement teaches the model the most.
- **Expected improvement** picks the location maximizing
  E[max(grade(x) − target, 0)] under the GP posterior with
  target = 0.05 oz/yd³. The idea is to drill where the model
  thinks there might be a hit *and* it's not sure.

Both criteria exclude points within 80 ft of an existing hole.

![Recommended next-drill locations on the GP-predicted grade surface. Cyan triangles maximize predictive variance (info gain). Magenta stars maximize expected improvement against a 0.05 oz/yd³ target.](data/derived/bear_cub_resource/fig_gp_drill_recommender.png)

The two criteria recommend different locations: max-variance picks
the gaps in the existing drill grid (largest empty spaces between
holes), while expected-improvement picks gaps that are also near
already-known high-grade holes. For an exploration program with a
budget for one more hole, the EI criterion is what most planners
would use; for a research campaign focused on understanding the
deposit's overall shape, max-variance is more informative.

**Limit.** The spatial-only GP doesn't predict pay-zone grade
accurately on n=24 holes. The leave-one-out R² is negative; the
model performs worse than just predicting the dataset mean. The
geological reason is that pay-zone grade has a sharp gradient at
the Third Beach Line crossing the property, and 24 spatial
samples can't constrain a gradient that steep. The recommender
output is **still meaningful even with a weak predictive model**:
it uses the GP's *uncertainty structure* (which is a faithful map
of where training data is sparse) rather than the GP's point
predictions. Max-variance picks remain the gaps in the grid; EI
picks remain gaps near known high-grade holes. The next hole
would help the GP fit the gradient.

## Interesting findings

**The historical record is internally inconsistent in ways that
matter.** Each row of each log has up to three different numbers
describing the same depth interval's gold content: the field
panner's color count plus rule-of-thumb weight estimate, the
supervisor's bench-scale weight after Wells/Janin displacement
correction, and a green-pencil reprice updating the historical
pennyweight value to a later gold price. The three numbers
describe different things, and the differences between them encode
real measurement uncertainty plus successive valuation layers. The
OCR pipeline preserves all three columns plus a derived
discrepancy flag rather than collapsing them.

**The pipeline generalizes to the rest of the Nome paleo-beach
archive.** Three concrete spinoff projects fall out of it: a
surveyor's toolkit
(four-corner WGS84 anchoring from a few GPS readings plus a patent
description), a drill-log CLI (folder of historical mining-record
scans to structured CSV with provenance and confidence flags per
cell), and a broader dark-data archive ingestion framework (court
filings, surveyors' notes, title histories, family correspondence,
all into one linked dataset).

## Deeper notebooks

- [Full Bear Cub project notebook](https://johnsondevco.com/plans/ai-minerals-internal/notebooks/bear_cub/main.html): pipeline, modeling, resource analysis with code.
- [Mining-engineer re-analysis](https://johnsondevco.com/plans/ai-minerals-internal/notebooks/bear_cub/mining_review.html): per-hole grades, plan view, resource estimate framed for a placer engineer.
- [Internal review notebook](https://johnsondevco.com/plans/ai-minerals-internal/notebooks/bear_cub/internal.html): caveats, TODOs, methodology audit trail.
- [Placer drill-log anatomy](https://johnsondevco.com/plans/ai-minerals-internal/notebooks/bear_cub/main.html#archive-and-conventions): how mid-twentieth-century churn-drill logs encode their data (Wolff + Wells references).
- [Bear Cub GP drill-site recommender](https://johnsondevco.com/plans/ai-minerals-internal/notebooks/drill_planning/bear_cub_gp_recommender.html): full notebook with both acquisition functions and the limits discussion.

[← back to portfolio map](index.qmd)
